did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes FFTL-Net, a deep learning model for skin disease classification. It utilizes a dense connection network (DenseNet) with feature fusion, transfer learning, and attention mechanisms to classify skin diseases using the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents FFTL-Net, a novel deep learning model for skin disease classification, and reports original experimental results including ablation studies, performance metrics (AUC, Accuracy, etc.) on the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets, and comparisons with other algorithms. It is labeled as a 'PAPER' in the journal Mach. Learn.: Sci. Technol., not a review.

title: FFTL-Net: a network for the classification of skin diseases based on feature fusion and transfer learning
brief_summary: This study proposes FFTL-Net, a deep learning model for skin disease classification that addresses data imbalance and overfitting through feature fusion and transfer learning. The architecture integrates a dense connection network with local binary pattern and HOG features, utilizing channel attention mechanisms and a class-balanced Softmax loss function. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets using five-fold cross-validation. FFTL-Net achieved an AUC of 98.16% on the ISIC 2018 dataset and 98.31% on the ISIC 2019 dataset, outperforming the second-ranked algorithms. The results demonstrate superior recognition accuracy and generalization capability, particularly for rare classes like BCC, with prediction accuracies of at least 93%.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets; mentioned as a malignant skin disease causing significant deaths; AUC > 99% reported for ISIC 2018.

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin disease categories (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Actinic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'classification of skin diseases'. The model FFTL-Net predicts discrete diagnostic categories using a class-balanced Softmax loss function. It was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets, which contain multi-class labels for various skin lesions. Performance metrics include AUC, Accuracy, Precision, F1, Sensitivity, and Specificity, all of which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge, containing dermatoscopic images of skin diseases. It is described as having 25,531 images in eight categories.

use.training: Used for model training via five-fold cross-validation on an 80% training-validation split.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning and model evaluation during the five-fold cross-validation process.
use.testing: Used for final model evaluation on a 20% test set.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 challenge, containing dermatoscopic images of skin diseases. It is described as larger than ISIC 2018, including SCC samples, with a total of 33,569 images.

use.training: Used for model training via five-fold cross-validation on an 80% training-validation split.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning and model evaluation during the five-fold cross-validation process.
use.testing: Used for final model evaluation on a 20% test set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the efficacy of the algorithm and evaluate model performance on the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets.

To obtain stable evaluation indices (Precision, F1, sensitivity, specificity) and assess the consistency and reliability of experimental results, particularly for classes with small sample sizes like Dermatofibroma (DF).
To report the average performance metrics and their variability across the 5 repeated experiments for FFTL-Net and comparison algorithms (e.g., AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy, F1).

To evaluate the model's ability to balance recognition rates across all classes and address data imbalance, specifically comparing the gap between micro-average and macro-average to assess performance on rare classes.
To measure the difference in data distribution between source and target domains in the domain adaptive strategy for transfer learning.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, ANOVA) for statistical significance. Performance comparisons were based on mean values and standard deviations from five repeated experiments, with superiority claimed based on higher metric values (e.g., AUC, Accuracy) rather than statistical significance tests.

ISIC 2018.notes: FFTL-net achieved an AUC of 98.16% and Accuracy of 93.40%, outperforming the second-ranked algorithm by 1.25% and 0.15% respectively. AUC values for NV, BKL, and VASC exceeded 99%, while the lowest AUC was for BCC at 93%. Micro-average and macro-average AUC differed by only 1%. Results are averages of 5 repeated experiments.

ISIC 2019.notes: FFTL-net achieved an AUC of 98.31% and Accuracy of 92.61%, outperforming the second-ranked algorithm by 0.33% and 0.71% respectively. Micro-average AUC reached 98% and macro-average AUC was 96%. AUC values for NV and VASC exceeded 99%. Average specificity for each category was more than 97.5%.

Ablation Study (ISIC 2019).notes: Removal of handcrafted features and attention mechanism led to substantial performance decline. Removal of class imbalance loss function resulted in substantial decline in F1 score. Domain adaptive strategy had a modest effect on final performance.

Backbone Network Comparison (ISIC 2019).notes: DenseNet-121 performed best. DenseNet-264 experienced overfitting due to increased complexity and parameters. DenseNet-121 AUC was 1.69% higher than ResNet-18 and 2-8% higher than other networks.

Loss Function Improvement (ISIC 2019).notes: Improvement resulted in an average increase of 0.04 in macro-average AUC and 0.09 in micro-average AUC. This indicates better balance in recognition rates across categories, particularly for small sample sizes like BCC.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning strategy based on domain adaptive strategy using Maximum Mean Difference (MMD) to measure distribution difference between source and target domains; specific pre-trained weights not explicitly named but DenseNet-121 is identified as the optimal backbone.

architecture_details.feature_fusion: Fusion of deep features from DenseNet with handcrafted features: Local Binary Pattern (LBP) and Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Channel attention mechanism integrated into the feature fusion module
architecture_details.domain_adaptation: Domain adaptive strategy based on Maximum Mean Difference (MMD)

training_details.validation_strategy: Five-fold cross-validation on 80% training-validation split, 20% test set

preprocessing_techniques: Normalizing pixel values to a range of 0–1
Gamma normalization (gamma correction with γ = 0.5) for illumination adjustment
Gradient calculation for HOG feature extraction (horizontal and vertical gradients)
Local Binary Pattern (LBP) feature extraction for texture description
conclusions: The proposed FFTL-Net model effectively addresses data imbalance, overfitting, and inadequate generalization in skin disease classification by integrating feature fusion (LBP and HOG with deep features), transfer learning, and a class-balanced Softmax loss function.
FFTL-Net achieved superior performance on the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets, with AUC values of 98.16% and 98.31% respectively, outperforming the second-ranked algorithms by 1.25% and 0.33%.
The model demonstrated robust recognition accuracy and generalization capability, particularly for rare classes such as Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), achieving prediction accuracies of at least 93%.

Ablation studies confirmed that the feature fusion module (combining handcrafted and deep features with attention mechanisms) is critical for maintaining high accuracy, while the class imbalance loss function significantly improved the F1 score for minority classes.

DenseNet-121 was identified as the optimal backbone network, offering a balance between performance and complexity, whereas deeper variants like DenseNet-264 suffered from overfitting due to the small and imbalanced nature of the skin disease datasets.

The integration of domain adaptive transfer learning and category equilibrium loss effectively balanced recognition rates across categories, reducing the gap between micro-average and macro-average AUC and improving performance on small sample sizes.
important_remarks: The study is limited to the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2019 datasets, which restricts the validation of the model's performance on random or external clinical datasets.
The paper explicitly states that further validation is required to assess performance on more extensive sets of skin disease data samples from hospitals.
The model's capacity to classify other skin disease categories beyond those in the ISIC datasets has not been validated due to space limitations.

The Dermatofibroma (DF) class has a very small sample size (22 specimens in ISIC 2018), leading to large fluctuations in experimental results, although the model claims robustness for rare classes like BCC.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; superiority was claimed based on mean metric values and standard deviations from five repeated experiments.
The model relies on transfer learning and domain adaptation strategies, which may introduce biases if the source domain distribution differs significantly from the target clinical distribution.

The paper does not report computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time) or deployment feasibility on edge devices, despite mentioning MobileNet and other lightweight architectures in comparisons.
